reviewNotes: Five medieval abstract works are represented by seven encoded texts: one Amoryus and Cleopes, two Palmistry witnesses, one Physiognomy, two Christmas-Day versions, and one Days of the Moon.

Craig's title pages, preface, contents, introduction, notes, glossary, and index are editorial publication layers. The pinned XML encodes the front matter, folds Craig's pp. 159-166 notes into the main text, and omits the glossary and index.
All Souls locators preserve both systems: Craig/Coxe old fols. 202r-212v correspond by textual boundaries to Watson current fols. 175v-185v. This is not treated as a simple arithmetic folio offset.
The Garrett cache is the complete 180-image Princeton IIIF object, not a crop to Metham's leaves. The work-specific canvas spans are locators inside that complete bundle.
Access, copyright status, rights statements, and provider reproduction terms are kept separate. Public viewing does not by itself establish open image rights.
Current MEDictionary XML preserves the core structure and counts but modernizes markup and differs substantively in two corrected readings; it is modeled as a later version of the U-M encoding.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is a pinned copy of the University of Michigan Humanities Text Initiative Metham encoding, created in 1999 from the 1974 Kraus reprint of Hardin Craig's 1916 EETS OS 132 edition. Craig transcribed Garrett MS 141 for Amoryus and Cleopes and the prose works, and printed Palmistry in parallel from Garrett and All Souls College MS 81. The pinned encoding contains Craig's front matter, a 2,211-line four-book romance, and six prose witness representations, but omits the printed glossary and index and integrates printed notes. Garrett 141 has a complete public 180-image Princeton facsimile. No complete public facsimile of All Souls MS 81 was verified; the current foliation crosswalk is established through Watson's complete 1997 catalogue. A current May 2026 MEDictionary XML is a descendant of the U-M item, not an upstream source of the pinned artifact.

catalogSummary.author: John Metham: author, translator, or compiler by work; the two Christmas-Day texts are attributed to him
catalogSummary.date: Amoryus and Cleopes composed in the twenty-seventh regnal year of Henry VI, 1 September 1448-31 August 1449; other work dates unresolved; collection first published 1916
catalogSummary.editor: Hardin Craig (1916 source edition); University of Michigan Humanities Text Initiative (1999 digital encoding)
catalogSummary.region: East Anglia, especially Norfolk; more specific places of composition are uncertain
catalogSummary.title: The Works of John Metham (Craig/U-M encoded collection)

catalogingSubject.label: The Works of John Metham (Craig/U-M encoded collection)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The edition-level collection represented by the pinned U-M/CME XML and delimited by Hardin Craig's 1916 EETS volume: Amoryus and Cleopes and four prose work families, with Palmistry represented from two witnesses and the Christmas-Day prognostication represented by a complete copy and a fragment. The collection contains five medieval abstract works but seven encoded textual representations. Craig's editorial front matter is retained as paratext with an item count of zero; the unencoded glossary and index are not medieval works. Manuscript witnesses, printed editions, facsimiles, and digital encodings are transmission layers described in the linked lineage manifest.

contentParts.label: Craig editorial paratext
notes: Craig's glossary (pp. 167-177) and index (pp. 178-184) are not encoded; their absence does not create additional medieval-work parts.
contentParts.summary: Encoded front matter: preface, contents, eight-section introduction, and the printed title opening Amoryus and Cleopes. It is publication-layer material, not a medieval work.

contentParts.label: Amoryus and Cleopes
notes: Craig reports two cancelled, illegible stanzas after the ending. A current METS web edition reports recovering erased material through modern imaging; that later intervention is not projected into the pinned XML or Craig's 1916 text.
contentParts.summary: A four-book Middle English verse romance adapting the Pyramus and Thisbe story and surviving uniquely in Garrett MS 141.

contentParts.label: First Book
contentParts.summary: The seventy-line prologue and first narrative book; U-M notes that the following division is conjectural and follows Craig.

contentParts.label: Second Book
contentParts.summary: The second top-level book division in Craig's edition.

contentParts.label: Third Book
notes: The XML has 757 L nodes here because two printed lines are split into duplicate-numbered nodes.
contentParts.summary: The third top-level book division, with its seven-line prologue.

contentParts.label: Last Book
notes: The XML subtree has 437 L nodes: 435 numbered nodes plus two quotation lines nested inside Craig's note at line 2193. Several printed lines are represented by split duplicate-numbered nodes.
contentParts.summary: The fourth and final book, including its twenty-eight-line prologue.

contentParts.label: Palmistry
contentParts.summary: Metham's Middle English translation of a Latin chiromancy text attributed in the tradition to Doctor Aurelian, printed in two full parallel witness versions.

contentParts.label: Palmistry: Garrett version
contentParts.summary: The fuller version from Garrett MS 141, used by Craig as the principal parallel text.

contentParts.label: Palmistry: All Souls version

locators.scheme: historical folios used by Craig and Coxe

contentParts.summary: A closely related full version from All Souls College MS 81, generally later in spelling and omitting two short paragraphs found in Garrett.

contentParts.label: Physiognomy
contentParts.summary: Metham's compilation and translation from Latin physiognomical traditions associated with Loxus, Polemon, Aristotle, and the Secretum secretorum.

contentParts.label: Christmas-Day Prognostications
contentParts.summary: Two separate Garrett representations of the Esdras-associated Christmas-Day prognostication tradition: one complete copy and one damaged flyleaf fragment.

contentParts.label: Christmas-Day Prognostication: complete Garrett version
contentParts.summary: The complete version in the main Garrett codex sequence.

contentParts.label: Christmas-Day Prognostication: Garrett flyleaf fragment
notes: Three XML L nodes belong to Craig's editorial note about the fragment, not to a verse work.
contentParts.summary: A second version on a spoiled sheet reused as a flyleaf; the text breaks off on the third line of its second page.

contentParts.label: The Days of the Moon
contentParts.summary: Metham's prose compilation of prognostications for lunar days in the lunationes tradition.

dateStatements.display: Twenty-seventh regnal year of Henry VI (1 September 1448-31 August 1449)
notes: The poem's regnal-year statement is more precise than a simple display date of 1449.

dateStatements.display: Mid-fifteenth century; exact work dates unresolved
notes: The Palmistry's reference to Metham's twenty-fifth winter does not supply a secure absolute date.

extent.description: Intellectual and representation metrics for the medieval content: five abstract works and seven encoded textual representations because Palmistry has two full witness versions and the Christmas-Day prognostication has a complete version and a fragment. Amoryus and Cleopes has 2,211 numbered intellectual lines in 319 stanza groups. The XML has 2,248 L nodes overall: 2,217 under the poem (including two editorial-note quotation lines), twenty-eight in the introduction, and three in the fragment note. Editorial paratext has item_count 0 and the unencoded glossary and index are excluded.

formStatements.description: The encoded collection combines one verse romance, six prose witness representations, and editorial paratext containing prose and quoted verse.

scope.description: Latin citations, source names, and formulae in medieval texts and editorial matter

notes: The All Souls folio systems are both retained: Craig and the older Coxe description use 202r-212v, while Watson's current foliation locates the same text at 175v-185v.

Raw XML L-node counts are encoding metrics, not authored line counts. Duplicate, split, truncated, and missing @N values prevent a naive node or number count from replacing Craig's canonical 2,211-line statement.
The U-M encoding integrates Craig's printed notes from pp. 159-166 into the text and omits those print pages as a separate section; it also omits the glossary and index.
The first/second book division after line 723 is conjectural in the electronic edition and follows Craig's organization.
Current MEDictionary XML is a descendant public encoding, not an upstream source of the pinned repository file.

next_steps: Compare the text against cataloged Latin Aurelian chiromancies and recent work on Middle English palmistry before assigning a normalized source-work identity.
openQuestions.question: What exact Latin chiromancy work and exemplar underlie Metham's Palmistry translation attributed to Doctor Aurelian?

next_steps: Consult recent source studies and compare distinctive chapter sequences against Latin witnesses.
openQuestions.question: Can Metham's exact Latin exemplars for the Physiognomy compilation be identified beyond Craig's broad Loxus-Polemon-Aristotle and Secretum secretorum source families?

notes: The text says Metham translated it in his twenty-fifth winter; this biographical age is not normalized into a calendar year.

scope.description: The Palmistry work represented by both Garrett and All Souls copies

notes: Craig identifies Latin physiognomical source traditions, but no exact exemplar has been established.
The two texts are not explicitly signed in the way that Metham's neighboring prose works are; attribution is retained as attributed rather than known.

scope.description: The complete and fragmentary Christmas-Day versions in Garrett 141
The 1916 collection, its transcription, supplied organization, notes, and front matter
Parallel Garrett and All Souls versions

summaries.value: The encoded collection joins John Metham's 2,211-line Amoryus and Cleopes with prose works on palmistry, physiognomy, Christmas-Day prognostication, and lunar-day prognostication. Palmistry appears in full parallel versions from Princeton Garrett MS 141 and All Souls College MS 81; the Christmas-Day work appears as a complete Garrett copy and a separate Garrett flyleaf fragment.

The five abstract medieval works, seven encoded witness representations, Craig's editorial paratext, two manuscript codices, and later edition and encoding layers are intentionally not flattened into one object.

notes: The qualifier makes the edition-level boundary visible and avoids treating the composite volume as one medieval work or a complete authorial corpus.

notes: Craig's edition title groups two full witness versions under this work label.

Palmistry appears in full parallel versions from Princeton Garrett MS 141 and All Souls College MS 81; the Christmas-Day work appears as a complete Garrett copy and a separate Garrett flyleaf fragment.
